frameworks.desc: 10-dimension scale for measuring the designerly mindset (2018).

frameworks.title: Osborn's Brainstorming Rules

activities.title: Empathy Workshop
activities.lede: A field-ready empathy map, journey map and stakeholder map sprint.

activities.title: Creative Sprint
activities.lede: A 90-minute diverge-converge cycle with live prioritisation and team voting.

activities.title: Strategy Canvas Lab
activities.lede: Teams draft a Business Model Canvas, overlay a DVF check, and stress-test with Porter's Five Forces.

activities.title: Uncertainty Challenge
activities.lede: Bird-in-hand, affordable loss and lemonade cards played against a live constraint.

activities.title: Opportunity Scan
activities.lede: JTBD interviews → Problem–Solution Fit → three testable experiments.

activities.title: Foundry Simulation
activities.lede: Stand up a fictional firm — legal form, cap table, runway, and first hire.

activities.title: Corporate Venture Lab
activities.lede: Design a 10-week intrapreneurship programme with sponsors, gates and demo day.

activities.title: Pitch Theatre
activities.lede: Distill → Decompose → Prioritise, then a 3-minute live pitch to an investor panel.

activities.title: Systems Mapping Lab · Capstone
activities.lede: A causal loop diagram of a wicked problem + a leverage-point intervention pitch.

prereadings.title: Wicked Problems in Design Thinking
prereadings.author: Richard Buchanan
prereadings.key_framework: Meadows' 12 Leverage Points in a System

questions: Recall a recent moment where you inferred what a user, customer or colleague wanted rather than asking them. What did you get right, and what did you miss?
Choose one product you use daily. Sketch a 5-step journey map of your last interaction with it — emotion line included. Where are the pain points?
If you had to run a 90-minute empathy workshop for your own team tomorrow, what is the one design challenge you would put at the centre of the room?
Recall the last time you said an idea out loud, then watched yourself quietly kill it before anyone else could. Which of the four fears was driving you?
Run a solo Crazy 8s session on a problem you currently own. Keep all 8 panels, even the bad ones. Which panels surprise you?
Most teams mix divergence and convergence in the same meeting and get muddy output. What one rule could you introduce tomorrow to prevent this in your team?
Take your current team's strategy (or your firm's). Plot it against the DVF triangle. Which corner is strongest? Which is weakest?
Pick one of the ten practices of strategy design. Describe a specific situation in which you have seen it done well, and one where you have seen it skipped entirely.
If you had to choose between a better plan and a better plan-adoption mechanism, which would make more difference in your organisation next quarter — and why?
Recall a moment in the last year when you treated an uncertain situation as if it were merely risky. What did the mistaken framing cost you?
Inventory your bird-in-hand today: who you are, what you know, who you know. Which of these three is most under-leveraged?
Define your affordable loss for an initiative you are currently considering. Write the number down. Does the exercise change your willingness to start?
Interview one person about the last significant purchase they made, using only open questions. What job were they hiring the product to do — and which dimensions did the product miss?
Pick one product in your firm's portfolio. If you took a JTBD lens, which competitors would suddenly appear on your radar that you currently ignore?
Christensen's framing has been in the management literature for two decades and is still under-used. Why do you think firms default back to demographic segmentation?

reflections.unit_title: Moving from an Idea to an Entrepreneurial Firm
questions: In your career so far, name one failure you would now classify as praiseworthy in Edmondson's sense. How was it treated at the time?
If you were stage-zero of your own venture tomorrow, what is the affordable loss — the maximum — you would write into your charter?

Wasserman's Founder's Dilemmas is built on a stark trade-off: rich or king — you can rarely be both. Which would you choose, and what does the answer reveal about why you would start a company at all?
Recall a time when you had an idea inside your organisation that should have happened and didn't. Which of the four success factors was missing?
If your CEO gave you a blank cheque and eighteen months to run any one intrapreneurship programme, which would you build — and who would be the first three people you would put in the room?
The hardest question for any intrapreneurship programme is not selection but re-entry: how successful ideas come back into the core business. Describe the re-entry mechanism you would design.
Can you state, in one sentence, what you do — and have the sentence be interesting? If not, which part of the Distill-Decompose-Prioritise protocol is breaking down?
Record yourself delivering your own 90-second pitch. Play it back. Where does the listener lose you — and is it a clarify problem or a convince problem?

Bostelaar and JD Meier's Thumbnail Thinking are deeply complementary: one tightens the message, the other designs its visual vehicle. Pick one of your Slibraries from this course and write the single sentence it condenses.
Name a wicked problem in your industry. Can you articulate it using Rittel & Webber's criteria — or do you find yourself reaching for simpler, solvable framings?
Meadows ranks parameter changes as low-leverage and paradigm changes as high-leverage. Most corporate initiatives operate at the parameter level. Why?

This course — MBA301 — has moved through nine units, 27 Slibraries and 135 frameworks. Which single framework do you now carry most reliably as a thumbnail you can pattern-match against under pressure? Why that one?
